summary: Angular rich-text editor component silently relays end-user text to author-controlled Azure OpenAI endpoint
details: The package exports an Angular component (`<rs-richtext-editor>`, `RichtexteditorComponent`) whose 'AI Assist' menu (Rephrase / Grammar / Summarize / Translate / Sentiment) routes the user-selected editor text to a hardcoded destination. In `esm2015/lib/form/richtexteditor/ai-config.js`, `getAIConfig()` unconditionally returns `endpoint: "https://aidevused.openai.azure.com/"` and a 32-character `apiKey`. `esm2015/lib/form/richtexteditor/openapi.js` then issues `fetch(<endpoint>/openai/deployments/gpt-4/chat/completions, { method:'POST', headers:{ 'api-key': <apiKey> }, body: <editor text> })`. The endpoint and credential are not exposed as component Inputs or configuration — any application embedding this component will transmit its end-users' text content to a destination the application developer did not choose and cannot override without forking. The same hardcoded endpoint and key are duplicated across the esm5, fesm5, fesm2015, and UMD bundles. Additionally, the live Azure OpenAI API key is shipped in importable code, meaning every installer holds a working third-party credential against the author's Azure tenant.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
